records.name: HIPAA Privacy Rule - Marketing Authorization and Notice of Privacy Practices

citation.act: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act; 45 CFR Part 164
citation.section: 45 CFR 164.508 (marketing authorization); 164.520 (Notice of Privacy Practices); 164.501 (marketing definition)

applies_when: Entity is a HIPAA covered entity (health plan, health care clearinghouse, or a health care provider that transmits health information electronically in connection with a covered transaction) or a business associate
Website uses protected health information (PHI) to market a product or service, or the provider must give patients a Notice of Privacy Practices
excluded_when: Entity is not a covered entity or business associate (for example a purely cash wellness business with no electronic covered transactions)
Communication falls within the treatment or care-coordination exceptions to the marketing definition in 45 CFR 164.501
De-identified data used per 164.514 (no PHI)

website_obligations.duty: Post a compliant Notice of Privacy Practices and link it from the site
elements: Link to Notice of Privacy Practices reachable from the homepage and any patient-facing page
Description of uses and disclosures, individual rights, and the entity duties
Effective date and a contact for privacy complaints

website_obligations.duty: Obtain valid HIPAA authorization before any use or disclosure of PHI for marketing
elements: No PHI-driven marketing (for example targeted email built from diagnosis or appointment data) without a signed 164.508 authorization
Authorization language discloses any remuneration where the marketing is paid for by a third party

penalty.basis: Tiered civil monetary penalties per violation, with an annual cap per identical provision (inflation-adjusted; the highest-tier per-violation maximum and the calendar-year cap are both 2,190,294 dollars for 2026); OCR resolution agreements common

regulator.name: U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, Office for Civil Rights (OCR)

enforcement.case: Gums Dental Care (OCR civil monetary penalty)
enforcement.summary: OCR imposed a 70,000 civil monetary penalty on a solo dental practice over a delayed patient records request, a signal that small practices are within enforcement reach.

intel.why_matters: A missing Notice of Privacy Practices or PHI used to build marketing lists is the most basic HIPAA failing OCR checks first, and it is visible on the public site.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Is a current Notice of Privacy Practices posted and linked, and can the entity show a signed authorization for any PHI used in marketing?
intel.relevance_hook: For a US covered entity the NPP link and marketing-authorization discipline are the first trust signals a regulator, and increasingly an AI answer engine, reads on the page.

records.name: HIPAA Online Tracking Technologies Guidance (OCR Bulletin) and Post-Vacatur Status

citation.act: HIPAA Privacy and Security Rules; 45 CFR Part 164; OCR guidance 'Use of Online Tracking Technologies by HIPAA Covered Entities and Business Associates'
citation.section: 45 CFR 164.502 (disclosures); 164.308/164.312 (safeguards); OCR bulletin Dec 2022, revised Mar 2024

applies_when: Entity is a HIPAA covered entity or business associate

Third-party tracking technologies (for example Meta Pixel, Google Analytics, advertising SDKs) run on authenticated pages such as a patient portal, or on pages where a user identifier is combined with information that reveals the individual received or sought care
excluded_when: Entity is not a covered entity or business associate

Tracking runs only on unauthenticated public pages and does NOT combine an identifier with health-condition or provider-visit information (the 'Proscribed Combination' theory for IP-plus-unauthenticated-condition-page was vacated by the N.D. Texas court in AHA v. Becerra, 20 June 2024, and HHS did not appeal)
Valid HIPAA authorization or a Business Associate Agreement covers the disclosure

website_obligations.duty: Prevent impermissible disclosure of PHI to tracking vendors on authenticated and PHI-bearing pages
elements: No third-party marketing or analytics trackers firing on patient-portal, appointment-booking, or symptom-check pages absent a BAA or authorization
Trackers on such pages inventoried and either removed or brought under a Business Associate Agreement
Server-side or de-identified analytics used where tracking is retained

penalty.basis: HIPAA civil monetary penalties where an impermissible disclosure occurs; separate class-action and FTC exposure runs far higher (see enforcement)

regulator.name: U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, Office for Civil Rights (OCR)

intel.why_matters: A tracking pixel on an appointment or portal page is the single highest-cost web-compliance failure in US healthcare, with aggregate settlement exposure across the hospital pixel-tracking litigation wave above 100m dollars, and it is detectable from network requests.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Which third-party trackers load on authenticated and appointment pages, and is each covered by a BAA or removed?
intel.relevance_hook: State it honestly: the court cut back part of the bulletin in June 2024, but the class actions and the FTC did not stop; the durable message is that PHI leaving through a pixel is still a live risk.

records.name: FTC Health Breach Notification Rule (2024 Final Rule)

citation.act: Federal Trade Commission Act; American Recovery and Reinvestment Act 2009 s.13407; 16 CFR Part 318
citation.section: 16 CFR Part 318 (as amended, final rule published 30 May 2024, effective 29 July 2024)

applies_when: Entity is a vendor of personal health records, a PHR-related entity, or a third-party service provider that is NOT covered by HIPAA
A health app or connected device draws identifiable health information from multiple sources (for example an app plus a device API or a synced account)
Unauthorized disclosure of identifiable health data occurs, including disclosure to advertising or analytics vendors via trackers or SDKs
excluded_when: Entity is a HIPAA covered entity or business associate acting in that capacity (HIPAA governs instead)
No identifiable health information is handled

website_obligations.duty: Do not disclose consumers' identifiable health data to advertising or analytics vendors without affirmative express consent
elements: No pixels or SDKs transmitting health-question answers, health interests, or purchase data to Meta, Google, TikTok and similar without opt-in
Clear privacy disclosure of any sharing

website_obligations.duty: Maintain a breach-notification capability and disclosure
elements: Ability to notify affected individuals, the FTC, and in some cases the media, of a breach of unsecured identifiable health data
Privacy policy reflects the notification commitment

penalty.basis: Civil penalty up to 53,088 dollars per violation per day (inflation-adjusted) for Rule violations; individual consumer-level counts drive very large totals

enforcement.summary: FTC's first Health Breach Notification Rule action: 1.5m civil penalty for disclosing health data to Facebook, Google, Criteo, Branch and Twilio through tracking pixels and SDKs, and a ban on sharing health data for advertising.

intel.why_matters: Non-HIPAA health apps and direct-to-consumer wellness sites wrongly assume they are unregulated; the HBNR reaches exactly the tracker-and-SDK sharing that is visible in their network traffic.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Does the app or site share any identifiable health data with third-party advertisers or analytics vendors, and did the consumer affirmatively consent?
intel.relevance_hook: The GoodRx penalty was built on the same pixels most direct-to-consumer health sites still run, which makes this an immediate, demonstrable finding.

records.name: FTC Act Section 5 - Unfair or Deceptive Practices (Health Data and Privacy Claims)
applies_when: Entity markets a health or wellness product or service to US consumers
Site makes privacy or security representations (for example 'HIPAA compliant', 'your information is private', a privacy seal) or shares consumer data in a way that contradicts its stated practices
excluded_when: No commercial marketing to US consumers
Purely internal or B2B communication with no consumer-facing claim

website_obligations.duty: Ensure privacy and security representations are truthful and not contradicted by actual data flows
elements: No unsupported 'HIPAA compliant' or privacy-seal claims
Data-sharing behaviour matches the privacy policy
Material connections and paid endorsements disclosed

penalty.basis: FTC Act Section 5 carries no general civil penalty for a first unfair/deceptive act; relief is consumer redress, disgorgement and injunctive bans. A civil penalty (up to 53,088 dollars per violation, 16 CFR 1.98, 15 U.S.C. 45(l)/(m)) attaches only where the conduct violates a prior FTC order or a specific FTC rule

enforcement.summary: 7.8m consumer refund and a ban on sharing health data for advertising, after disclosing email, IP and mental-health questionnaire data to Facebook, Snapchat, Criteo and Pinterest despite privacy promises.
Order over sharing sensitive consumer data with LinkedIn, TikTok and other platforms, illustrating the BetterHelp and GoodRx enforcement pattern for telehealth and mental-health brands.

intel.why_matters: The BetterHelp and Cerebral cases turned ordinary marketing pixels plus a privacy promise into multi-million-dollar orders; the deceptive gap is between the stated policy and the actual data flows.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Do the site privacy claims (including any 'HIPAA compliant' badge) match where consumer data actually goes?
intel.relevance_hook: An unbacked 'HIPAA compliant' seal or a privacy policy contradicted by live trackers is a clean, provable Section 5 exposure.

records.name: Americans with Disabilities Act Title III - Website Accessibility

citation.act: Americans with Disabilities Act; 28 CFR Part 36
citation.section: 42 U.S.C. 12181 et seq.; DOJ web accessibility guidance (2022) referencing WCAG 2.1 AA

applies_when: Entity is a place of public accommodation, which includes the professional office of a health care provider (42 U.S.C. 12181(7)(F))
Website offers goods, services, appointment booking, or patient information to the US public
excluded_when: Purely private club or a US government entity (Title II, not Title III)
No US public-facing web presence

website_obligations.duty: Make the public-facing website accessible, in practice to WCAG 2.1 AA
elements: Text alternatives for images, labelled form fields, sufficient colour contrast
Full keyboard operability and no keyboard traps
Captions for media; accessible booking and portal flows
Published accessibility statement describing conformance and a contact

penalty.basis: Private suits seek injunctive relief plus attorney fees (settlements commonly tens of thousands); DOJ civil penalties in government-initiated Title III actions are inflation-adjusted to up to 118,225 dollars for a first violation and 236,451 for subsequent violations (28 CFR 85.5, referenced by 28 CFR 36.504, effective 3 July 2025)

regulator.name: U.S. Department of Justice, Civil Rights Division (plus private litigants)

enforcement.summary: 1m order for claiming an AI accessibility widget could make any website WCAG-compliant and for formatting paid reviews as independent; confirms an accessibility overlay is not a defence to Title III.

enforcement.case: Robles v. Domino's Pizza (9th Cir.; cert. denied)
enforcement.summary: Leading authority that ADA Title III applies to the websites of public accommodations, the engine behind thousands of annual web-accessibility suits.

intel.why_matters: Health-provider sites are frequent ADA web-suit targets, and an overlay widget does not cure it; a genuine WCAG 2.1 AA pass plus a statement is the defensible posture.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Does the site meet WCAG 2.1 AA in practice and publish an accessibility statement, or is it relying on an overlay?
intel.relevance_hook: Automated accessibility failures (missing alt text, unlabelled booking forms, contrast) are objective, demonstrable findings that map straight to litigation risk.

records.name: HHS Section 504 Rule - Web and Mobile Application Accessibility

citation.act: Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973; 45 CFR Part 84
citation.section: 45 CFR 84.84 (web and mobile accessibility, WCAG 2.1 AA); final rule published 9 May 2024

applies_when: Entity is a recipient of HHS federal financial assistance (for example a hospital, clinic, or provider receiving Medicaid, Medicare Part A, or HHS grants)
Entity operates a public-facing website or mobile application
excluded_when: Entity receives no HHS federal financial assistance
Content types within the rule's specific exceptions (for example certain archived or pre-existing conventional electronic documents)

website_obligations.duty: Bring web content and mobile apps into conformance with WCAG 2.1 Level AA by the applicable compliance date
elements: WCAG 2.1 AA conformance across public web content and mobile apps
Remediation plan and accessibility statement
Attention to booking, portal, and forms flows

penalty.basis: Enforced through OCR administrative action and the federal-funding condition; non-compliance can jeopardise HHS funding and trigger complaints. No fixed monetary maximum in the rule

regulator.name: U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, Office for Civil Rights (OCR)

intel.why_matters: This is a hard, HHS-specific digital-accessibility mandate distinct from ADA Title III, tied to federal funding, and it reaches most hospitals and clinics.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Is the site on a documented path to WCAG 2.1 AA by the applicable Section 504 compliance date?
intel.relevance_hook: State the date honestly: the compliance deadline for recipients with 15 or more employees was extended from 11 May 2026 to 11 May 2027 (and to 10 May 2028 for smaller recipients), but the WCAG 2.1 AA obligation itself stands.

records.name: FDA 21 CFR Part 202 - Prescription Drug Advertising (including online and DTC)

citation.act: Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act; 21 CFR Part 202

applies_when: Entity advertises or promotes a prescription drug to US audiences online, including product pages, social posts, and paid search
excluded_when: No prescription-drug promotion (for example a provider site that names no branded Rx product for promotional purposes)
Scientific exchange or approved labelling reproduced without promotional claims

website_obligations.duty: Present prescription-drug promotion with fair balance and required disclosures
elements: Established (generic) name accompanies the brand name
Fair balance of risk and benefit information, not benefit-only
Only on-label, substantiated claims; no unapproved uses
Access to a brief summary or a clear link to Full Prescribing Information

penalty.basis: FDA (OPDP) issues untitled and warning letters requiring corrective action; misbranding can lead to injunction, seizure and criminal prosecution. No standard civil-penalty schedule for this provision

regulator.name: U.S. Food and Drug Administration, Office of Prescription Drug Promotion (OPDP)

intel.why_matters: Benefit-heavy Rx promotion without fair balance or a Prescribing Information link is the classic OPDP warning-letter trigger and is visible on the product page.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Does the promotional page carry fair balance, the established name, and a route to Full Prescribing Information, with no off-label claims?
intel.relevance_hook: Fair balance and a Full Prescribing Information link are binary checks; their absence on an Rx product page is an immediate FDA-promotion flag.

records.name: FDA Medical Device Promotion and Misbranding (FD&C Act)

citation.act: Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act; 21 CFR Parts 801 and 807
citation.section: FD&C Act s.502 (21 U.S.C. 352) misbranding; 21 CFR 801 (labelling); intended-use claims

applies_when: Entity markets a medical device (including software as a medical device) to US audiences online and makes claims about its performance, safety, or intended use
excluded_when: Product is not a device, or claims are strictly general-wellness within FDA's low-risk general-wellness policy
No promotional intended-use claims that would establish device status or off-label use

website_obligations.duty: Keep device promotion consistent with the cleared or approved intended use and truthful
elements: Claims limited to the device's cleared or approved intended use; no promotion of unapproved uses
Required labelling and directions accessible; no false or misleading performance claims
Consistency between marketing claims and FDA clearance or approval status

penalty.basis: FDA warning letters, seizure, injunction and criminal prosecution for misbranding; FTC may act in parallel on deceptive claims. No standard civil-penalty schedule for the misbranding provision

regulator.name: U.S. Food and Drug Administration, Center for Devices and Radiological Health (CDRH)

intel.why_matters: Aesthetic, wellness and digital-health sites routinely make device claims that exceed the cleared intended use, which is a misbranding and FTC risk visible in the copy.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Do the device claims match the FDA-cleared or approved intended use, and are any performance claims substantiated?
intel.relevance_hook: Off-label or exaggerated device claims on a treatment or product page are a concrete, quotable finding.

records.name: FTC Health-Claims Substantiation Doctrine

citation.act: Federal Trade Commission Act; FTC Health Products Compliance Guidance (2022); FTC Policy Statement on Advertising Substantiation (1984)
citation.section: 15 U.S.C. 45; competent and reliable scientific evidence standard

applies_when: Entity makes objective health, disease, treatment, or fitness claims to US consumers (for example 'clinically proven', 'reduces pain', before-and-after results)
excluded_when: No objective health or performance claims (pure opinion or general branding)
Claims backed by, and consistent with, competent and reliable scientific evidence with proper disclosures

website_obligations.duty: Hold competent and reliable scientific evidence for every objective health claim before it is published
elements: Substantiation (generally well-conducted human clinical evidence) on file for each claim
No 'clinically proven' or comparative superiority claim without the matching evidence
Testimonials reflect typical results or carry a clear typicality disclosure; material connections disclosed

penalty.basis: Injunctive relief, consumer redress, disgorgement and corrective advertising; FTC Act Section 5 carries no general civil penalty. A civil penalty (up to 53,088 dollars per violation, 16 CFR 1.98, 15 U.S.C. 45(l)/(m)) attaches only where a prior FTC order or a specific FTC rule is breached

intel.why_matters: Unsubstantiated 'clinically proven', 'guaranteed', and atypical before-and-after claims are the most common health-marketing violation and are readable straight off the page.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Can the advertiser produce competent and reliable scientific evidence for each objective claim, and do testimonials carry typicality disclosures?
intel.relevance_hook: Phrases like 'clinically proven' or 'permanent results' with no citation, and undisclosed before-and-after galleries, are direct, quotable substantiation findings.

records.name: State Medical Board Advertising Rules - California

citation.act: California Business and Professions Code; Medical Board of California
citation.section: Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code 651 (false, misleading or deceptive advertising by healing-arts licensees; a violation of s.651 is a misdemeanor); 2271 (advertising in violation of the false-advertising law is unprofessional conduct and grounds for licence discipline)

applies_when: Physician or medical practice licensed in California, or advertising professional medical services to California residents
Website makes claims about qualifications, specialties, board certification, treatment outcomes, or patient testimonials
excluded_when: Not a licensed medical practice and not advertising medical services in the state
Purely factual, accurate practice information with no comparative or outcome claims

website_obligations.duty: Keep all advertising truthful, non-misleading, and properly substantiated
elements: No false, misleading or deceptive statements about services or outcomes
Accurate board-certification and specialty statements (no implying certification the physician does not hold)
No unsubstantiated superiority claims ('best', 'guaranteed', 'painless', 'permanent cure')
Patient testimonials and before-and-after images carry required disclosures

penalty.basis: State-board disciplinary action for unprofessional conduct under B&P s.2271 (licence suspension or revocation, administrative fines); false or misleading advertising under B&P s.651 is separately a misdemeanor; amounts vary by board

regulator.name: Medical Board of California (and the Attorney General for injunctive relief)

intel.why_matters: State boards, alongside the FTC, act on misleading treatment claims and inaccurate board-certification statements; the risky language sits on the homepage.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Are the board-certification statements accurate and are outcome or superiority claims substantiated?
intel.relevance_hook: For a California-licensed practice, superlatives and unqualified 'board-certified' or 'guaranteed' claims are a licence-level exposure, not just a marketing quibble.

records.name: State Medical Board Advertising Rules - New York

citation.act: New York Education Law; Rules of the Board of Regents
citation.section: N.Y. Educ. Law 6530(27); 8 NYCRR 29.1(b)(12) (advertising or soliciting not in the public interest, including false, deceptive or misleading advertising)

applies_when: Physician or medical practice licensed in New York, or advertising professional medical services to New York residents
Website makes claims about qualifications, specialties, board certification, treatment outcomes, or patient testimonials
excluded_when: Not a licensed medical practice and not advertising medical services in the state
Purely factual, accurate practice information with no comparative or outcome claims

website_obligations.duty: Keep all advertising truthful, non-misleading, and properly substantiated
elements: No false, misleading or deceptive statements about services or outcomes
Accurate board-certification and specialty statements (no implying certification the physician does not hold)
No unsubstantiated superiority claims ('best', 'guaranteed', 'painless', 'permanent cure')
Patient testimonials and before-and-after images carry required disclosures

penalty.basis: New York State Board for Professional Medical Conduct disciplinary action for professional misconduct (false, deceptive or misleading advertising under N.Y. Educ. Law 6530(27) / 8 NYCRR 29.1(b)(12)). Penalties under N.Y. Public Health Law 230-a: censure and reprimand, licence suspension, limitation, revocation or annulment, and a fine not exceeding USD 10,000 upon each specification of charges of which the licensee is found guilty; no fixed website-specific tariff. Verified on N.Y. Public Health Law 230-a (official) 2026-07-17.

regulator.name: New York State Board for Professional Medical Conduct / Office of the Professions

intel.why_matters: State boards, alongside the FTC, act on misleading treatment claims and inaccurate board-certification statements; the risky language sits on the homepage.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Are the board-certification statements accurate and are outcome or superiority claims substantiated?
intel.relevance_hook: For a New York-licensed practice, superlatives and unqualified 'board-certified' or 'guaranteed' claims are a licence-level exposure, not just a marketing quibble.

records.name: State Medical Board Advertising Rules - Texas

citation.act: Texas Occupations Code; Texas Administrative Code (Texas Medical Board rules)
citation.section: Tex. Occ. Code 164.052(a)(6) (false, misleading or deceptive advertising); 22 TAC Chapter 164 (Physician Advertising)

applies_when: Physician or medical practice licensed in Texas, or advertising professional medical services to Texas residents
Website makes claims about qualifications, specialties, board certification, treatment outcomes, or patient testimonials
excluded_when: Not a licensed medical practice and not advertising medical services in the state
Purely factual, accurate practice information with no comparative or outcome claims

website_obligations.duty: Keep all advertising truthful, non-misleading, and properly substantiated
elements: No false, misleading or deceptive statements about services or outcomes
Accurate board-certification and specialty statements (no implying certification the physician does not hold)
No unsubstantiated superiority claims ('best', 'guaranteed', 'painless', 'permanent cure')
Patient testimonials and before-and-after images carry required disclosures

penalty.basis: Texas Medical Board disciplinary action for false, misleading or deceptive advertising under Tex. Occ. Code 164.052(a)(6): administrative penalties, licence restriction, suspension or revocation; amounts are set by the board

intel.why_matters: State boards, alongside the FTC, act on misleading treatment claims and inaccurate board-certification statements; the risky language sits on the homepage.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Are the board-certification statements accurate and are outcome or superiority claims substantiated?
intel.relevance_hook: For a Texas-licensed practice, superlatives and unqualified 'board-certified' or 'guaranteed' claims are a licence-level exposure, not just a marketing quibble.

records.qa_note: Downgraded to needs_verification (PR #3 round-2, 2026-07-17): the disciplinary ground Tex. Occ. Code 164.052(a)(6) (false, misleading or deceptive advertising) is corroborated only on secondary sources, and the board's penalty authority (Tex. Occ. Code 164.001 methods of discipline; Ch. 165 administrative penalties) was NOT content-verified because the official Texas Statutes site (statutes.capitol.texas.gov) returns a client-rendered navigation shell rather than the section text to automated fetching. Excluded from the compiled artifact until the penalty consequence is confirmed on the official Texas Statutes / Texas Medical Board source. No fabricated figure shipped (amounts null).

records.name: State Medical Board Advertising Rules - Florida

citation.act: Florida Statutes; Florida Administrative Code (Board of Medicine)
citation.section: Fla. Stat. 458.331 (disciplinary grounds for physicians) and 456.062 (advertising of free or discounted services); penalties under Fla. Stat. 456.072(2); Rule 64B8-11 F.A.C. (advertising)

applies_when: Physician or medical practice licensed in Florida, or advertising professional medical services to Florida residents
Website makes claims about qualifications, specialties, board certification, treatment outcomes, or patient testimonials
excluded_when: Not a licensed medical practice and not advertising medical services in the state
Purely factual, accurate practice information with no comparative or outcome claims

website_obligations.duty: Keep all advertising truthful, non-misleading, and properly substantiated
elements: No false, misleading or deceptive statements about services or outcomes
Accurate board-certification and specialty statements (no implying certification the physician does not hold)
No unsubstantiated superiority claims ('best', 'guaranteed', 'painless', 'permanent cure')
Patient testimonials and before-and-after images carry required disclosures

penalty.basis: Florida Board of Medicine disciplinary action for false, deceptive or misleading advertising under Fla. Stat. 458.331. Penalties under Fla. Stat. 456.072(2): an administrative fine not to exceed USD 10,000 for each count or separate offence, plus reprimand or letter of concern, probation, practice restriction, licence suspension or permanent revocation; amounts are set by the board and there is no fixed website-specific tariff. Content-verified on Fla. Stat. 456.072 (official) 2026-07-17.

regulator.name: Florida Board of Medicine (Department of Health)

intel.why_matters: State boards, alongside the FTC, act on misleading treatment claims and inaccurate board-certification statements; the risky language sits on the homepage.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Are the board-certification statements accurate and are outcome or superiority claims substantiated?
intel.relevance_hook: For a Florida-licensed practice, superlatives and unqualified 'board-certified' or 'guaranteed' claims are a licence-level exposure, not just a marketing quibble.

records.name: State Medical Board Advertising Rules - Illinois

citation.section: 225 ILCS 60/22 (disciplinary grounds, including false or misleading statements about skill or the efficacy or value of treatment)

applies_when: Physician or medical practice licensed in Illinois, or advertising professional medical services to Illinois residents
Website makes claims about qualifications, specialties, board certification, treatment outcomes, or patient testimonials
excluded_when: Not a licensed medical practice and not advertising medical services in the state
Purely factual, accurate practice information with no comparative or outcome claims

website_obligations.duty: Keep all advertising truthful, non-misleading, and properly substantiated
elements: No false, misleading or deceptive statements about services or outcomes
Accurate board-certification and specialty statements (no implying certification the physician does not hold)
No unsubstantiated superiority claims ('best', 'guaranteed', 'painless', 'permanent cure')
Patient testimonials and before-and-after images carry required disclosures

penalty.basis: Illinois Medical Disciplinary Board (IDFPR) disciplinary action under 225 ILCS 60/22 for false or misleading statements about skill or the value of treatment: reprimand, fines, and licence suspension or revocation; amounts are set by the board

regulator.name: Illinois Department of Financial and Professional Regulation (Medical Disciplinary Board)

intel.why_matters: State boards, alongside the FTC, act on misleading treatment claims and inaccurate board-certification statements; the risky language sits on the homepage.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Are the board-certification statements accurate and are outcome or superiority claims substantiated?
intel.relevance_hook: For a Illinois-licensed practice, superlatives and unqualified 'board-certified' or 'guaranteed' claims are a licence-level exposure, not just a marketing quibble.

records.qa_note: Downgraded to needs_verification (PR #3 round-2, 2026-07-17): the ground 225 ILCS 60/22 (false or misleading statements about skill or the efficacy or value of treatment) and the IDFPR / Medical Disciplinary Board penalty authority were NOT content-verified because the official Illinois General Assembly source (ilga.gov) refused the connection from the verification environment (ECONNREFUSED) and no alternative official host serves the Illinois Compiled Statutes. An unreachable source is not disproof, but the per-state penalty consequence must not ship as candidate unverified. Excluded from the compiled artifact until 225 ILCS 60/22 and the board's penalty are confirmed on ilga.gov / IDFPR. No fabricated figure shipped (amounts null).

records.name: Telehealth State Licensure Requirement (Advisory)

citation.act: State medical practice acts (licensure where the patient is located); Federation of State Medical Boards guidance
citation.section: State-specific medical practice acts; the practice of medicine is generally deemed to occur where the patient is located

applies_when: Entity or clinician provides telehealth to patients located in US states
Website advertises nationwide or multi-state availability, or lists conditions treated for patients in specific states (for example CA, NY, TX, FL, IL)
excluded_when: Services limited to states where every treating clinician is licensed or validly registered
Care that falls within a recognised state exception (for example physician-to-physician consultation or follow-up continuity of care)

website_obligations.duty: Do not represent availability of care in states where clinicians are not licensed or registered
elements: Claims of 'available in all 50 states' or state lists match actual clinician licensure or telehealth-registration coverage
State-availability logic in the booking flow gated to licensed states
No implication of a treatment relationship the clinician cannot lawfully form in that state

penalty.basis: Practising without a licence in the patient's state is unlicensed practice: state-board cease-and-desist, fines and, in some states, criminal exposure; amounts vary by state

intel.why_matters: Most state medical practice acts locate the practice of medicine where the patient sits, so a 'nationwide' telehealth claim can assert unlicensed practice in states the clinicians do not cover.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Do the states named or implied on the site match the states where the treating clinicians actually hold a licence or telehealth registration?
intel.relevance_hook: An 'available in all 50 states' banner that the licence footprint cannot support is a demonstrable state-availability overclaim, especially across CA, NY, TX, FL and IL.

records.name: 21st Century Cures Act - Information Blocking and Patient Access

citation.act: 21st Century Cures Act; ONC interoperability and information-blocking rule; 45 CFR Part 171
citation.section: 45 CFR Part 171 (information blocking and exceptions); PHSA s.3022; disincentives rule 45 CFR Part 171 subpart / 42 CFR (2024)

applies_when: Entity is an 'actor' under the rule: a health care provider, a health IT developer of certified health IT, or a health information network or exchange
Entity operates a patient portal or otherwise controls patient access to electronic health information (EHI)
excluded_when: Entity is not an actor under 45 CFR 171.102
Practice fits one of the eight information-blocking exceptions (for example the Privacy, Security, or Infeasibility exceptions)

website_obligations.duty: Do not interfere with patients' timely, no-cost electronic access to their health information through the portal
elements: Patient portal provides access to EHI without unnecessary delay
No fees or friction that would constitute information blocking for standard patient access
Portal access and rights signposted; delays justified only by a recognised exception

penalty.basis: Health IT developers and HIEs/HINs face civil monetary penalties up to 1,000,000 dollars per violation; health care providers face 'appropriate disincentives' set by HHS rather than CMPs

regulator.name: HHS Assistant Secretary for Technology Policy / ONC and the HHS Office of Inspector General (OIG)

intel.why_matters: Portals that delay or charge for patient access, or bury the access route, risk an information-blocking finding, and this is the patient-access signal AI and regulators expect a modern provider site to carry.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Can patients get timely, no-cost electronic access to their records through the portal, without practices that would count as information blocking?
intel.relevance_hook: A patient-portal access path that is missing, delayed, or fee-gated is a concrete Cures Act signal on the site.

records.name: Washington My Health My Data Act (Consumer Health Data)
applies_when: Entity conducts business in Washington or targets Washington consumers and collects 'consumer health data' (broadly defined, including data that identifies a consumer's health status, conditions, or attempts to seek care)
Entity is NOT acting as a HIPAA covered entity for that data
excluded_when: Data is HIPAA protected health information or otherwise exempt under RCW 19.373
Entity does not collect consumer health data or does not reach Washington consumers

website_obligations.duty: Publish a separate consumer health data privacy policy and obtain consent before collection or sharing
elements: Distinct 'Consumer Health Data Privacy Policy' linked on the homepage
Affirmative, separate consent before collecting consumer health data and again before sharing it
No selling of consumer health data without a valid written authorization
No geofencing around health-care facilities to trigger ads or collect data

penalty.basis: Enforced under the Washington Consumer Protection Act; violations are actionable by the Attorney General and via a private right of action, with CPA remedies

regulator.name: Washington State Attorney General (plus private right of action)

intel.why_matters: MHMDA reaches direct-to-consumer health and wellness sites that fall outside HIPAA, and it demands a distinct policy plus consent that most sites do not have; the private right of action raises the stakes.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Is there a separate consumer health data privacy policy, and is consent obtained before any consumer health data is collected or shared?
intel.relevance_hook: A missing standalone Consumer Health Data Privacy Policy and pre-consent trackers are visible, provable MHMDA gaps for any site reaching Washington users.
